title: Topic 345: Why New IPs Are Risky: The Psychology of the Unknown
content:
## ABSTRACT

This paper bravely ventures into the treacherous territory of new intellectual properties (IPs) in the video game industry, a domain where fortunes are won and lost with the roll of a polyhedral die. We posit that the human brain, a marvel of evolution that has successfully avoided being eaten by saber-toothed tigers for millennia, is fundamentally lazy and risk-averse. This inherent neurological disposition, which we affectionately term ‘cognitive comfort-seeking,’ leads to a demonstrable preference for the familiar over the novel. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of psychological principles such as ambiguity aversion and the mere-exposure effect, we demonstrate why gamers, despite their vocal cries for innovation, will flock to the 17th installment of ‘Generic Space Marine Shooter’ while giving a wide berth to ‘Avant-Garde Interpretive Dance Simulator.’ This paper will explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, supported by real-world examples from the gaming world, and conclude that launching a new IP is less a calculated business decision and more a form of high-stakes corporate gambling, albeit with fewer free drinks.

## INTRODUCTION

Imagine, if you will, that you are standing before a grand buffet. On your left, a steaming, succulent platter of your favorite dish, a meal you’ve enjoyed a thousand times, a guaranteed source of gustatory pleasure. On your right, a mysterious, glowing, gelatinous cube. It might be the most delicious thing you’ve ever tasted, a symphony of flavors that will redefine your culinary landscape. Or, it could be a sentient, raspberry-flavored amoeba that will give you a nasty case of existential indigestion. Which do you choose?

If you’re like most people, you’ll probably stick with what you know. And that, in a nutshell, is the central dilemma facing the video game industry. For years, a vocal contingent of gamers has been clamoring for novelty, for fresh ideas and daring new worlds to explore. Yet, when faced with the choice between a familiar franchise and a bold new IP, the market often gravitates towards the former. This paper, therefore, seeks to answer a fundamental question: why are new IPs so damn risky? Is it a failure of marketing? A lack of quality? Or is there something deeper, something hardwired into our very psychology, that makes us shy away from the unknown?

We will argue that the answer lies in the latter. Our brains, those magnificent, three-pound lumps of anxiety and pattern-recognition, are inherently conservative. They are finely tuned to assess risk and seek comfort in the familiar. This paper will delve into the psychological literature on risk aversion, ambiguity, and cognitive biases to explain why we, as consumers, are often our own worst enemies when it comes to innovation. We will explore how these psychological tendencies manifest in the video game market, with a close look at both triumphant successes and spectacular failures. Finally, we will discuss the broader implications of our findings for understanding consumer behavior in an age of endless choice and crippling uncertainty. So, grab your +5 Vorpal Sword of Critical Analysis, and let’s venture forth into the unknown. What’s the worst that could happen?

## LITERATURE REVIEW

Before we embark on our own intellectual adventure, it is prudent to consult the wisdom of those who have traversed these perilous lands before us. The academic landscape is littered with the bleached bones of failed theories and the triumphant banners of established psychological principles. Our journey begins with the concept of **ambiguity aversion**, a cognitive bias first described by the economist Daniel Ellsberg in 1961. Ellsberg demonstrated that people overwhelmingly prefer to bet on known probabilities rather than unknown ones, even if the odds are objectively the same. In other words, we’d rather play a game with a 50% chance of winning than a game where the odds are a complete mystery. This is not because we are irrational, but because our brains are wired to treat uncertainty as a threat. As the saying goes, “better the devil you know than the devil you don’t.”

This fear of the unknown is a recurring theme in psychological research. A 2016 paper by R. Nicholas Carleton, aptly titled “Fear of the unknown: One fear to rule them all?”, argues that this fundamental fear may underlie a wide range of anxiety disorders. From an evolutionary perspective, this makes perfect sense. Our ancestors who blithely wandered into dark, unexplored caves were more likely to become a prehistoric predator’s lunch than those who stuck to the well-trodden path. This deep-seated aversion to uncertainty has been a successful survival strategy for millennia, and it continues to shape our decisions in the modern world, from our choice of romantic partners to our selection of video games.

Further compounding this issue is the **mere-exposure effect**, a psychological phenomenon by which people tend to develop a preference for things merely because they are familiar with them. First identified by Robert Zajonc in the 1960s, this effect has been demonstrated in a wide range of contexts, from our preference for certain words and symbols to our liking of particular faces. The more we see something, the more we like it. This is why advertisers spend billions of dollars bombarding us with the same logos and jingles until they are seared into our neural pathways. In the context of video games, this means that a well-established franchise has a significant built-in advantage over a new IP. The very familiarity of its characters, world, and gameplay mechanics makes it more appealing to our comfort-seeking brains.

Finally, we must consider the role of **cognitive dissonance**, the mental discomfort experienced by a person who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values. When we are presented with a new IP, we are forced to confront the possibility that our existing preferences might not be optimal. This creates a state of cognitive dissonance, which we are motivated to resolve. The easiest way to do this is to simply reject the new IP and reaffirm our commitment to our established favorites. This is not a conscious process, but rather an automatic, self-preservation mechanism of the ego. It is the psychological equivalent of sticking our fingers in our ears and humming loudly to drown out any dissenting opinions.

## ANALYSIS

Now that we have armed ourselves with the theoretical weaponry of modern psychology, let us turn our attention to the blood-soaked battlefield of the video game industry. The average AAA game now costs hundreds of millions of dollars to produce and market, a sum that would make a small nation-state blush. With such astronomical stakes, it is no wonder that publishers are hesitant to gamble on unproven concepts. A new IP is a leap of faith into a financial abyss, a high-risk, high-reward proposition that can either lead to a glorious new franchise or a catastrophic write-down.

The psychological principles we have discussed provide a powerful framework for understanding why this is the case. The ambiguity aversion of consumers means that a new IP starts with a significant disadvantage. It is an unknown quantity, a mystery box that could contain either a masterpiece or a buggy, unplayable mess. A sequel, on the other hand, is a known quantity. We know the characters, we know the world, and we have a pretty good idea of what the gameplay will be like. It is the safe bet, the comfortable choice for our risk-averse brains.

The mere-exposure effect further reinforces this dynamic. A franchise like *Call of Duty* or *FIFA* has been a part of the cultural landscape for decades. Its iconography is instantly recognizable, its gameplay loops are deeply ingrained in the muscle memory of millions of players. A new IP, no matter how brilliant, simply cannot compete with this level of familiarity. It is a whisper in a hurricane, a flickering candle in a supernova of established brand recognition.

This is not to say that new IPs are doomed to fail. On the contrary, the history of gaming is filled with examples of bold new ideas that have captured the hearts and minds of millions. However, these successes are often the exception rather than the rule. They typically require a perfect storm of factors: a visionary developer, a compelling concept, a flawless execution, and a healthy dose of good luck. For every *Minecraft* or *World of Warcraft*, there are a dozen *Anthems* or *LawBreakers*, ambitious new IPs that crashed and burned on the unforgiving altar of the market.

The economic realities of the industry also play a significant role. The rise of the “games as a service” model has made long-term player engagement the holy grail of modern game development. Publishers are no longer interested in selling a single, standalone experience. They want to create a platform, a hobby, a recurring revenue stream that will keep players hooked for years to come. This business model is far better suited to established franchises than to new IPs. A sequel already has a built-in audience, a community of players who are invested in the world and its characters. A new IP has to build that community from scratch, a monumental task in an increasingly crowded and competitive market.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Let us now descend from the lofty heights of academic theory and get our hands dirty with some real-world examples. The annals of gaming history are a treasure trove of cautionary tales and triumphant underdog stories. Consider the case of *Anthem*, a much-hyped new IP from the acclaimed developer BioWare. The game promised a thrilling, *Iron Man*-esque experience in a lush, alien world. The marketing budget was colossal, the expectations were sky-high. And yet, upon release, the game was met with a resounding thud. It was a critical and commercial disappointment, a hollow shell of a game that failed to deliver on its ambitious promises. What went wrong? While the game was plagued by a host of technical issues and design flaws, its failure can also be attributed to the psychological factors we have discussed. It was a new IP, an unknown quantity, and it simply could not compete with the established giants of the genre like *Destiny* and *Warframe*.

In stark contrast, we have the phenomenal success of *Among Us*, a simple, low-budget game that became a global sensation in 2020. The game was not a new IP in the traditional sense, as it had been released two years prior to its explosion in popularity. However, for the vast majority of players, it was a completely new experience. Its success can be attributed to a number of factors, including its accessibility, its social nature, and its perfect timing during a global pandemic that left people craving human connection. But it also demonstrates that a new idea, if it is compelling enough, can overcome the inherent biases of the market. *Among Us* was a breath of fresh air in a stale and stagnant genre, a testament to the power of innovation and the enduring appeal of a good old-fashioned witch hunt.

Another interesting case study is that of *No Man’s Sky*, a game that was both a spectacular failure and a remarkable success. At launch, the game was a colossal disappointment, a victim of its own hype and the impossible expectations of its creator. It was a vast, procedurally generated universe that was ultimately empty and devoid of meaning. The backlash was swift and brutal. And yet, against all odds, the developers at Hello Games refused to give up. They spent the next several years releasing a series of massive, free updates that transformed the game into the sprawling, ambitious experience it was always meant to be. Today, *No Man’s Sky* has a dedicated and passionate community, a testament to the power of redemption and the enduring appeal of a good comeback story. It is a powerful reminder that even a failed new IP can be resurrected if the developers are willing to put in the work.

## DISCUSSION

The challenges facing new IPs in the video game industry are not unique. They are a reflection of a broader psychological truth: we are creatures of habit, drawn to the comforting embrace of the familiar. This same dynamic can be observed in a wide range of consumer behaviors. We tend to frequent the same restaurants, buy the same brands of toothpaste, and watch the same tired sitcoms, not because they are objectively the best, but because they are familiar. They are the safe choices, the cognitive shortcuts that allow us to navigate an increasingly complex and overwhelming world.

This has profound implications for innovation in all its forms. It means that any new idea, whether it is a new product, a new service, or a new work of art, faces an uphill battle against the entrenched forces of habit and familiarity. It must not only be good, it must be so compellingly, undeniably, life-changingly good that it can overcome our innate resistance to change. This is a tall order, and it is why true innovation is so rare and so precious.

However, this is not to say that we are doomed to a future of endless sequels and reboots. The human spirit is a restless and curious thing. We may be drawn to the familiar, but we also crave novelty and excitement. The success of games like *Elden Ring* and *Baldur's Gate 3*, both of which are new IPs (or at least significant departures from their predecessors), demonstrates that there is still a massive appetite for bold, ambitious, and innovative experiences. The key is to strike the right balance between the familiar and the new, to offer a taste of the unknown without completely overwhelming our comfort-seeking brains.

Ultimately, the psychology of the unknown is a double-edged sword. It is the source of our greatest fears and our most profound anxieties, but it is also the wellspring of our creativity, our curiosity, and our insatiable desire to explore. The challenge for creators in all fields is to understand this fundamental tension and to use it to their advantage. It is to create experiences that are both comforting and challenging, familiar and surprising, safe and, just maybe, a little bit dangerous.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the perilous journey of a new IP in the video game industry is a microcosm of the human condition itself. We are torn between the comforting embrace of the known and the siren song of the unknown. Our brains, those magnificent, anxiety-ridden survival machines, are hardwired to prefer the former, to seek refuge in the familiar and to view the novel with suspicion. This is why sequels and franchises dominate the market, and why true innovation is such a rare and precious commodity.

And yet, despite the overwhelming odds, new ideas continue to emerge, to challenge our preconceptions, and to push the boundaries of what is possible. For every dozen generic shooters, there is a *Disco Elysium*, a *Hades*, or an *Outer Wilds*, a game that dares to be different, that takes a risk, and that reaps the rewards of its audacity. These are the games that remind us why we fell in love with this medium in the first place. They are the beacons of hope in a sea of sameness, the shining examples of what can be achieved when creativity and courage triumph over the cold, hard calculus of the market.

So, the next time you find yourself standing before that metaphorical buffet, torn between the familiar and the unknown, I urge you to take a chance on the glowing, gelatinous cube. It might be a disaster. It might give you a nasty case of existential indigestion. But it also might be the most amazing thing you’ve ever experienced. And isn’t that worth the risk?
references:
- Ellsberg, D. (1961). Risk, ambiguity, and the Savage axioms. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 75(4), 643-669.
- Carleton, R. N. (2016). Fear of the unknown: One fear to rule them all?. Journal of anxiety disorders, 41, 5-21.
- Zajonc, R. B. (1968). Attitudinal effects of mere exposure. Journal of personality and social psychology, 9(2p2), 1.
- Festinger, L. (1957). A theory of cognitive dissonance. Row, Peterson.
